courses.subject: GDRS
courses.title: Thinking Gender: An Introduction to Gender Studies
courses.description:
(UGEC1209 is double-coded with GDRS1001.)

This course aims to develop students’ ability to analyze the everyday life through the lens of gender. Drawing on the interdisciplinary nature of Women’s and Gender Studies, the course will guide students to examine how gender works as one of the primary social organizing principles in the movies we watch, the housework we do, the behaviors we express and many other daily activities that have been routinized. The first three lectures will equip students with a critical gender lens by questioning biology and getting familiar with theories of gender role, socialization, gender schema, doing gender and queer theory. All concepts to be explored in class will be explained with reference to research. At the end of the course, students will come to understand how political their personal life is. In particular, the course hopes to inspire students to take part in creating social change for gender equality, which is in line with the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals.

courses.description:
The course offers a more advanced exploration of key strands of feminist thought. Rather than reinforcing a static and often oversimplified portrait of feminism through the categories of first, second, and third waves, it invites you to engage with the debates that continue to shape contemporary feminism. We will examine selected foundational texts, appreciate their intellectual contributions and influence, and critically assess how far they provide us with tools to understand our present moment and envision meaningful social change in times of crisis. Students will read a range of works by theorists advancing diverse feminist perspectives and analyze the strengths and limitations of each. The course aims to familiarize students with the diversity of feminist theories and to introduce key issues on which feminists diverge.

A blended-mode section is available for this course. Please refer to the “Class Notes” of that section for further details.

courses.subject: GDRS
courses.title: Quantitative Research Methods in Gender Studies
courses.description: This course introduces students to quantitative research methods and skills in Gender Studies. Students will be exposed to a variety of quantitative methodologies, and recognize gender bias in the application of research methods. The course adopts a combination of lectures, class discussion, and analytical practices. We will go through the different phases in the research process of formulating research questions, designing measurements and hypotheses, choosing of methods for accessing sources, conducting data analysis, and writing reports. Students will develop the critical awareness of quantitative methods through discussion and application of research methods and be prepared to design their own research project in the real setting with the quantitative research methods they learn in class. They will be able to reflect upon the choice and application of research methods from a gender perspective in their own research.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon successfully completing this course students should be able to:
1. Learn basic concepts and procedures of quantitative research methods and understand the logic of quantitative approaches in social analyses and in Gender Studies
2. Construct quantitative research with appropriate measurements and methods
3. Present quantitative research findings in an appropriate form orally and in writing
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction

2. Research design: taking gender into consideration

3. Conceptualization from a gender perspective

4. Survey research and feminist survey research

5. Sampling and collecting data

10. Writing quantitative research

courses.subject: GDRS
courses.title: Qualitative Research Methods in Gender Studies
courses.description:
The qualitative research methods are an essential skill in the contemporary world and are widely used in gender studies. Having the gender lens in qualitative research lends itself greater importance as it helps to uncover the mechanism and the intersectional social forces giving shape to forms of inequalities, identities, and communities. As AI and big data are the buzz words today to restructure the future work force, qualitative research skills are becoming increasingly important in a world that are gradually governed by machines. People are not satisfied with merely algorithmic associations but want to know the meanings, emotions, sensibility, and identities behind data. Qualitative methods in gender studies capacitate us to engage in deep reflection about the process, dynamics, and the deeper structure of the social world to come up with a better solution to many problems of inequality.

Gender studies is an interdisciplinary field, but in this course, we will focus on the qualitative research methods used by feminist inquires in the social sciences. Students will be exposed to a variety of qualitative research methods from fieldwork to beyond. Epistemology of gender research and what is considered feminist research will be introduced. We will cover important qualitative cases and the associated ethical issues. In order to understand the process of applying qualitative research methods, students will apply the knowledge learned in class through designing and conducting their own mini qualitative research project. They will report the progress of their research in class through group presentations or assignments to receive constructive comments from the instructor and fellow students. Respect and tolerance of different voices are important to the course.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken PSYC2070 and SOCI3231.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Understand the fundamental concepts, process, and ethics of qualitative research methods
2. Learn about different types of qualitative research methods that are commonly applied in gender studies
3. Frame research questions and employ research methods suitable for the research
4. Becoming reflexive of the subject and researcher’s position in qualitative research
5. Design and conduct research by collecting and analyzing data
6. Develop the skills for writing and presenting a qualitative research proposal and research

courses.subject: GDRS
courses.title: Gender Studies: Special Topic II
courses.description:
This course conducts selected studies on some important topics in Gender Studies. The course may cover topics such as gender and sexuality issues in different cultural, political and social contexts, and with historical and comparative perspectives.
The focus of the course may vary according to the area of expertise of the instructor and the interests of the class.
The learning activities enable students to acquire and implement design thinking innovative skills. They are required to apply the skills and knowledge to address gender and sexuality issues in a local/global context, and self-reflect on the efforts/outcomes of their products.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon successful completion of this course, the student will:

1. have a solid grasp on the history and theories related to Gender Studies

2. recognize the plurality and diversity of academic debate on Gender Studies through identifying and analyzing the issues in the subject area

3. acquire a basic understanding of the multi-faceted range of Gender Studies

4. comprehend current affairs critically and analytically through convergent and divergent thinking

5. Apply the skills, values and knowledge in a local/global context

courses.subject: GDRS
courses.title: Gender Studies: Field Study
courses.description:
The course is conducted by the teaching staff from this Programme. The content of this course varies from semester to semester, but concentrates either on selected problems related to the encounter with other societies through experiential fieldwork, or on special issues in Gender Studies. Contents of this course will vary with instructors and students.

Students are required to join all lectures and workshops to show their capacities in applying those theories, particularly the research skills and methodologies, learnt from different courses. They are requested to put their ideas into practice by engaging with the communities outside Hong Kong. It also aims to strengthen the relationship between teachers, the society, external stakeholders and students so that students’ global citizenship is established and strengthened. It can also provide opportunities for the students to work in a team. They are required to submit an individual report afterwards.

courses.subject: GDRS
courses.title: Love and Intimate Relationship
courses.description:
(UGED3208 is double-coded with GDRS3004.)

This course reviews basic concepts, theories and research findings of romantic love and intimate relationship. Various aspects of romantic love and intimate relationship will be critically reviewed. Based on research from a wide range of disciplines including gender studies, cultural studies, sociology, psychology, counselling, media studies, among various fields, together with input from literature and popular culture, the course will cover major areas such as the following: how do we understand the notions of “love” and “intimacy” in the different social and personal contexts, what are the different stages in a relationship; the influence of cultural myths and social scripts; interpersonal processes within romantic relationships (such as attraction, social cognition, communication and conflicts, etc.); how social gender role shapes romantic feelings and behaviors; notions of equality and freedom within relationships; power and violence; intimate relationship across life span, etc.

Students are encouraged to actively reflect upon their own life experience, to engage in critical discussions of cultural phenomenon through case studies, and learn to untangle the cultural myths, norms, and stereotypes that shapes our private lives and our society in general.

Upon the completion of the course, students should demonstrate basic understanding of structure, functions, dynamics, and development of romantic love and other interpersonal relationship. The personal is political. As personal relationships are the most basic social relationships that makes up the larger network of social relationships, it is hope that students could use knowledge from this course as an insight to understand, develop, and improve the quality of their own interpersonal relationship, and would aspire to build a more open and equal society starting from equalitarian and mindful personal relationships.

Through critical exercise and personal reflection on notions and experience of Love and Intimate Relationship in relations to notions such as power, diversity, equality, and freedom, the course engages directly with the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) adopted by all United Nations Member States in 2015 by engaging directly with the SDGs 5 (Gender Equality), 10 (Reduced Inequalities) and 16 (Peace, Justice, and Strong Institution).
courses.course_attributes:
SDG-GE #5 Gender Equality
SDG-GE #10 Reducing Inequality
SDG-GE #16 Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions
courses.learning_outcomes:
Expected Learning Goals

1. Understand the knowledge base of romantic love and intimate relationship

2. Be equipped with the research methods in romantic love and intimate relationship from multiple disciplines such as psychology, cultural studies, sociology, philosophy and anthropology

3. Relate the knowledge on love and intimate relationships that they learn from the course to their everyday life

4. Develop their own values on love and intimate relationships after considering relevant theoretical views and empirical research

5. Gain insights about romantic love and intimate relationship

6. Critically reflect on gender and other inequalities in society and interpersonal relationships

Learning Outcomes

·Understand, relate and apply the key romantic love and intimate relationship concepts to daily life

·Critically engage with gender and other inequalities in society and interpersonal relationships

·Familiarize with the key classical and contemporary research findings in romantic love and intimate relationship

·Conceptualize, structure, articulate and present original ideas, both in written assignments and during tutorial discussions, on romantic love and intimate relationship related topics

·Design, direct and regulate own study plan for problem-based learning, from identifying the problem, setting learning objectives, researching on relevant information, reflecting on what has been learnt, to applying the knowledge to future problems

courses.subject: GDRS
courses.title: Living Feminisms: Creating a Nonviolent and Collaborative Future
courses.description:
(UGEC3204 is double-coded with GDRS3006.)

Living Feminisms is all about how to put feminism into practice. Specifically, it is concerned with living out feminist consciousness in every step of making fundamental and lasting social change. With this in mind, Living Feminisms is not an exposition of feminist theories per se but an exploration of how social change effort would look like when we take up feminist consciousness that transcends separateness and duality. It explores the root causes that lead to the domination system that has plagued humanity for centuries, examines the possibility of having a fundamental and lasting social transformation that supports life of all, and takes a closer look at a few selected alternative practices such as restorative justice and sharing economies that hold out hope in times of crises.

The course also equips students with practical knowledge of being a change agent, introducing some practices with feminist consciousness that transform limiting beliefs about social change, nourish resilience by taking care of social and political trauma, and cultivate need-based facilitation skills and leadership that help navigate conflicts in the process of making internal as well as external transformation. To put it in a nutshell, Living Feminisms is a course that cultivates change agents with feminist consciousness.

Living Feminisms deeply resonates with the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) adopted by all United Nations Member States in 2015 as a blueprint for building a sustainable future by 2030. The course directly touches on alternative practices working towards the SDGs 5 (Gender Equality), 8 (Decent Work and Economic Growth), 10 (Reduced Inequalities), 11 (Sustainable Cities and Communities), 16 (Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions) and 17 (Partnerships for the Goals).

courses.learning_outcomes: Upon completion of the course, students will acquire both knowledge and skills of social change that are in line with feminist consciousness. Specifically, they will be able to explain the roots of the domination system and a series of factors contributing to its perpetuation, and able to make change by transforming internal and external resistance. Students will be empowered to take lead in change effort through collaboration with others and mastery of conflict transformation skills. Students will learn to appreciate making a sustainable future requires transformation in areas broader than the conventionally defined economic realm.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Class 1: How We Got Where We Are and Where Are We Heading?**

·Riane Eisler and Douglas P. Fry’s biocultural approach that explains the perpetuation of domination systems through the interaction between genes, brain development and culture, and points to ways where hope lies

* *Class 2: How Change Happens? A Lesson from Feminists**

·Investigating over 100 organizations in 15-year longitudinal research, Aruna Rao et al. develop a model of change that sheds lights on the mechanism of social transformation

·Cristina Bicchieri’s work delves into the formation and change of norms

* *Class 3: What Makes Things Stuck? Inaction Explained**

·How can we explain inaction of people witnessing immense suffering of others? Is the notion of false consciousness adequate? Joanna Macy, a Buddhist scholar and a veteran environmentalist, offers us some glimpse

* *Class 4: *Practice*: Retelling a New Story and Reconnecting to Life that We All Share**

·Understanding the impact of separateness at cognitive level is important but far from complete, Sharif Abdullah and Gretchen Ki Steidle offer us some practical advice on healing the deep-seated separateness by retelling a new story about human beings that fosters interdependence

* *Class 5: Taking Action: Violent or Nonviolent or Both?**

·Taking action to initiate change at various levels of a society is commonplace for activists. What is important is the kinds of action we undertake What is nonviolence? How does it work? What are its limits?

* *Class 6: Social Trauma and Social Transformation**

·Trauma is an overwhelming experience that outweighs the containing capacity of our nervous system How does trauma relate to and shape our political behaviors? Rather individualizing trauma as a single person experiences, how can we theorize collective trauma?

* *Class 7: *Practice*: Self-Care & Inner Resources**

·Change agents are prone to feel despair, powerlessness and hopelessness because they are more acutely aware of the enormity of suffering How to nourish our heart to make it open to pain and suffering while keeping the passion alive?

* *Class 8: Reconciliation and Justice: Exploring Transitional Justice**

·Common collective responses to atrocities are either forgetting or vengeance. Is transitional justice a viable third way? How does it work? What are the experiences of the 40 Truth and Reconciliation Commissions that have ever set up in human history can tell us?

* *Class 9: Sexual Violence: Exploring Restorative Justice**

·Our current justice system is a kind of retribution that punishes those who violate the law. How does it look like if we shift from retribution to restoration that focuses on needs and rebuilding of broken relationships? How controversial is it when applying to sexual violence?

* *Class 10: Collaboration and Feminist Leadership: Coming to Terms with Power**

·Activism often entails working with others. Is power necessarily evil? How does feminist leadership look like? How can we consciously use power in a way that does not subjugate but empower others? How can we replace power-over with power-with, power-to and power-within? Power-cube analysis

* *Class 11: Alternative Organizing - Holacracy, Sociocracy and Deep Democracy**

·There are always implicit agreements as to how to make decisions, define the nature and the scope of a task, evaluate outcome, and handle conflicts if we do not consciously decide how and what to decide

·Quite a many grassroots organizations have been experimenting with new ways of organizing that is more efficient than representative democracy in meeting the needs of all. These alternative forms of governance include Holacracy, Sociocracy and Deep Democracy.

* *Class 12: *Practice*: Workshop on Convergent Facilitation**

·Convergent facilitation, developed by the feminist Miki Kashtan, a form of facilitation that caters for both willingness and efficiency in order to come up an agreement, decision and/or concrete strategies that maximally work for the needs of all within a group. The workshop will cover the whole process from the start to the end convergent facilitation is a proven one as it was applied to a group who disagreed with each other about legalization of abortion

* *Class 13: Beyond Neoliberalism? Sharing Economy**

·Our current economies are profit-driven that more often than not serves a few but exploits most of other people Is it really hopeless to revise capitalism? Is Sharing Economy a viable alternative?

* *Class 14: Conflict Transformation: From the Personal to the International**

Disagreements and conflicts among activists are commonplace. However, acquiring ways of resolving and transforming conflicts within groups is a skill that is urgently needed but seldom given enough attention this last lecture will touch on conflict resolution and mediation.

courses.title: Understanding Masculinities and Social Change
courses.description:
(UGEC3229 is double-coded with GDRS3007.)

This course examines men’s diverse experiences as boys/men and public discourses and practices about masculinities. It will focus on how the gendered social order influences men’s actions and the way men perceive themselves, other men, women, and social situations. It will pay special attention to the exploration of the relationships between multiple dimensions of social relations and inequalities including gender, race/ethnicity, class, and sexual orientation. It will address issues including but not limited to: male socialization, boyhood, male body images, male friendship, male sexuality and fertility, men’s experiences as fathers, male aggression and violence, men’s commitments to work, men’s social participation, and media representations of boys and men in contemporary culture. It will also explore other forms and interpretations of masculinities, such as female masculinities and queer masculinities, and to investigate to what extent they post challenges to hegemonic masculinity. Students will be introduced to the latest developments and their global networks, and to evaluate the prospects for social change in how men may think, feel, and act otherwise.

The course resonates with the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) adopted by all United Nations Member States in 2015 by engaging directly with the SDGs 5 (Gender Equality) and 10 (Reduced Inequalities), and 16 (Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions) through critical engagement with different discourses and phenomenon related to masculinities.

courses.course_syllabus:
* *Week 1**

* *Introduction - Thinking about masculinities**

Key ideas: Introducing critical Masculinities Studies as a field of study, and introduce the history of the development of the discipline

SDG: 5 (Gender Equality) and 10 (Reduced Inequalities), and 16 (Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions)

* *Week 2**

* *Theorizing masculinities (1): Classic theories**

Key ideas: Introduce classic theories of Masculinities Studies, and familiarise students with important arguments and debates

SDG: 5 (Gender Equality) and 10 (Reduced Inequalities)

* *Week 3**

* *Theorizing masculinities (2): New developments**

Key ideas: Based on classic theories, trace debates and new developments in the field

SDG: 5 (Gender Equality) and 10 (Reduced Inequalities)

* *Week 4**

* *Education, “salary man” and the delusion of the “masculine promise”: Understanding masculinity in East Asia amidst social-economic change**

Key ideas: Starting with education of boys, talk about the systematic creation of certain idealised mode of “masculinity”, often closely related to life planning along occupational line. Using the case of “salary man” of post-war Japan and Korea, explore the “masculine promise” in East Asia is challenged amidst social-economic change

SDG: 5 (Gender Equality) and 10 (Reduced Inequalities), and 16 (Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions)

* *Week 5**

* *Masculinity, organization, the masculinization of social change**

Key ideas: Explore the notions of institutionalised masculinity and political masculinity, and how it shapes imaginations of social institutions and social change

SDG: 5 (Gender Equality) and 10 (Reduced Inequalities), and 16 (Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions)

* *Week 6**

* *Construction of a collective identity: Masculine imaginations of swordsmen and gangsters in Hong Kong cinema**

Key ideas: Using the case of the swordsmen and gangster character in Hong Kong cinema, explore the construction of collective identity along a matric of masculine imaginations of the Hong Kong subject

SDG: 5 (Gender Equality) and 10 (Reduced Inequalities), and 16 (Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions)

* *Week 7**

* *What makes a man: Penis, dildos, and the challenges of the disembodied Phallus**

Key ideas: Explores the centrality of libido, virility, and sexual performance in the construction and validation of masculinity, and its problems and challenges

SDG: 5 (Gender Equality) and 10 (Reduced Inequalities)

* *Week 8**

* *Men on both sides of the sex industry**

Key ideas: Explore how the roles men play in the sex industry, both as sex workers, and consumers of commercialised sex, and its implications on different practices of masculinity

SDG: 5 (Gender Equality) and 10 (Reduced Inequalities)

* *Week 9**

* *Mental wellness, self-harm, emotional literacy, and the unique challenges of emotional support for men**

Key ideas: Explore the topic of mental well-being, and its complex relationship with masculinity – how certain beliefs about masculinity could potentially reduce mental well-being, and how culture is increasingly empowering men to have increased emotional literacy and means of expression

SDG: 5 (Gender Equality) and 10 (Reduced Inequalities)

* *Week 10**

* *“Boys’ Love” and the reimagining male emotional and sexual potentiality**

Key ideas: Explore how boys’ love culture is opening up cultural space for discussions, explorations, and reimaginations of male emotional and sexual potentiality

SDG: 5 (Gender Equality) and 10 (Reduced Inequalities)

* *Week 11**

* *Masculine ideal and the policing of queer Asian possibilities**

Key ideas: Explore how the centrifugal force of certain dominant notion of masculinity is actively shaping the possibilities of different racial and sexual bodies

SDG: 5 (Gender Equality) and 10 (Reduced Inequalities), and 16 (Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions)

* *Week 12**

* *Not your cis-hetero-men: Masculinities according to trans man, butches, and drag kings**

Key ideas: Explore how different actors in society are complicating and challenging normative definitions and practices of masculinity, opening up space for discussions, explorations, and reimaginations of multiple masculinities

SDG: 5 (Gender Equality) and 10 (Reduced Inequalities), and 16 (Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions)

courses.subject: GDRS
courses.title: Family and Society
courses.description:
(UGEC3224 is double-coded with GDRS3009.)

This course examines issues revolving the family as a social institution in different cultures. It explores processes of how the family is constructed in different forms as it intersects with various social systems including kinship, marriage, class, ethnicity, religion, and political economy. Particular attention is paid to how family as a gendered construction, while being a basic principle of social life globally, exhibits great diversity as it shapes and is shaped by local histories, traditions, and state policies. In an era of globalization and transnational mobility, the ideology and practice of the family are rapidly changing, but there are simultaneously trends of reversion and localization. Students will read ethnographies and develop cross-cultural perspectives on classic and contemporary issues concerning the family, such as work and domesticity, motherhoods and fatherhoods, sexual orientations, reproductive technologies, surrogacy and adoption. Students learn to critically think and articulate about family issues, and will acquire enhanced knowledge and skills in reflexive thinking and positive communication, cultural sensitivity, and diversity and inclusion.

The course is relevant to the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) adopted by all United Nations Member States in 2015, in particular SDGs 5 (Gender Equality), 10 (Reduced Inequalities) and 16 (Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions).

courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completion of the course, students will be able to:

1. describe anthropologically the different forms of family and the reasons they exist in different cultures, and have a broad-base understanding and worldview of the diversity of human experiences.
2. engage in contemporary debates in relation to the definition of family, same-sex parenthood and reproductive technologies, and to develop skills to dissipate stereotypes and discriminatory practices regarding sexual orientations.
3. analyze issues surrounding domesticity and violence against women in different cultural settings, and investigate potential solutions for gender inequality and evaluate barriers to related problems in their own society.
4. reflect on and apply concepts of cultural relativism, and to analyze ethnocentrism and explore ways to terminate racial discrimination.
5. reflect on their ability and steps to take as change-makers and agents of justice and peace.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. The Family: An anthropological perspective (SDG #5, 10, 16)

The family is often imagined to be a social institution with a set of values and relationships shared by everyone in society. This is far from reality. In this lecture we look at the definitions of family across cultures, and look at how family interacts with different social systems such as religion, political system and modes of production, and investigate how family values may differ in different ethnic groups and how they change over time. Concepts of cultural relativism and holism will be introduced.

2. Kinship and lineage (SDG #5, 10)

Kinship and lineage have played an important part in the political economy of society, in the past as well as in present time. In this lecture we discuss the commonalities as well as the differences across cultures, and look at the factors leading to continuity and/or change, such as urbanization, globalization, appearance of reproductive technologies, adoption, surrogacy, and changing values in sexuality.

3. Role of marriage in family (SDG #5, 10, 16)

This lecture looks at how marriage may or may not be related to family formation, and the role of marriage in society. In addition, marriage regulations and practices are highly related to gender ideologies. These relations exist in diverse forms and have transformed over time, for example as governments promulgate new marriage laws and as civil society demands for reform.

4. Domesticity and gender division of labor (SDG #5, 10, 16)

How was the idea “man are breadwinners; women are homemakers” formulated in history and how is it challenged in contemporary society? This lecture will discuss issues of domesticity and how gender division of labor at home is related to the gender division of labor in society. We will examine the roles of religion and science, as well as of the church and government in the production of gendered social arrangements. Students will learn to identify systemic barriers to gender equality.

5. Violence against Women (SDG #5, 16)

Why are women the majority of victims of domestic violence across cultures? Why do many women choose to stay in abusive relations? In this lecture we look at the social and cultural factors that influence women’s decision to confront, escape, or endure domestic violence, and discuss how family and ideologies and practices have played a part in the perpetuation of VAW in different cultures. Students will investigate potential solutions for VAW as a form of gender inequality.

6. Parenthood and socialization (SDG #5, 10)

Do all parents love their children? Do parents in different cultures raise their children the same way? Why is raising children in families with same-sex parents, single parents, and migrant parents considered problematic? If socialization is the way to reproduce socially acceptable individuals, what form, content, and means should it take? This lecture seeks to dispel the misconception that parent-child relations are natural and uniform, by looking at the diverse forms of parenthood and ways of socialization in different cultures.

7. Spousal relations: duties, affection, and intimacy (SDG #5, 10)

How is spousal relationship culturally constructed? What are the components of spouse-hood and how do they relate to politics, economics, religion and other social systems? Why do extra-marital affairs happen and how does society deal with it? This lecture looks at the ideology and practice of spouse-hood across different cultures, and asks what role it has in formulating the family as social institution.

8. Globalization, affluence and livelihood (SDG #5, 10, 16)

This lecture looks at global transformation processes and how they affect the family both as a social institution and as a network of related individuals. We look at how consumerism, social media, and converging values seemingly sync-ing familial structures and emotions across the globe, but at the same time noting how families in different societies, as they undergo globalization and wealth polarization, are torn by desires of economic growth and downward social mobility.

9. Transnational mobility and family (SDG #5, 10)

In an era of globalization, more and more people cross national borders for a better life, creating transnational families as family members live in different countries in the form of split households, or as individuals become members of family in a different culture. In this lecture we look at this growing phenomenon and discuss issues about acculturation, family relations and power dynamics, how affines and agnates create transnational communities, and what happens to those who are left behind in the hometown.

10. New kinship and alternative family (SDG #5, 10)

We will discuss how arrangements such as same-sex marriage, cohabitation, and polygamous relations would challenge conventional understandings of kinship and family in society. We will reflect on these issues in relation to contemporary debates on heterosexual normativity, monogamy, and same-sex relations.

11. Doing family in China (SDG #5, 16)

The concept of family has various levels of meanings in Chinese culture, and throughout Chinese history these have transformed in response to the needs and ideologies of the time. While Han families may share certain idealized views of the family as a social system, its practice is perhaps as varied as the number of families. Ethnic groups and Chinese overseas communities add to this complexity as their worldviews, religions, and socioeconomic environments acculturate thinking and behavior that differ from what is understood as Chinese core values.

12. Doing family in Asia (SDG #5, 16)

This lecture will bring together ethnographic cases in Asia to illustrate the interaction of different social systems revolving around the family. By comparing changes and continuities of family formation and relations in the region using a cross-cultural perspective, we are able to reflect on our own culture, and understand the pros and cons of the ideologies and practices that we are socialized in.

13. Family, power, and intersectionality (SDG #5, 10, 16)

This lecture discusses individuals’ multi-layered identities and how these intersect with family membership. We will examine roles, statuses and power generated from positions in the family, gender and ethnicity, at work and school, on the media and more. The multiple locales in which individuals perform their everyday experiences are examined holistically, which helps us rethink how seemingly “natural” behavior are really cultural constructs, and how taken-for-granted ideas of family membership shape our everyday lives, our life goals as individuals, and the regulations and resources that constitute society.

courses.subject: GDRS
courses.title: Advancing Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion in Organizations
courses.description: The past few years have seen a growing recognition of the importance of diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) in organizations. This course aims to introduce students to the tools and frameworks needed to approach DEI from a leadership perspective. Students will gain a deep understanding of the organizational, cultural, and intergroup contexts in which DEI initiatives arise, which DEI initiatives work and why, the drivers that motivate DEI initiatives, and the opportunities and barriers to building effective DEI initiatives in organizations. The course is designed to help students navigate diverse organizational settings more effectively, work within and lead diverse teams, and apply DEI frameworks to address organizational challenges. The course also provides students with the opportunity to critically discuss topics such as identity, discrimination, micro-aggressions, and equity in society, and how these themes relate to organizational issues of power, privilege, opportunity, inclusion, innovation, and organizational effectiveness. By the end of the course, students will have the knowledge and skills to be inclusive leaders and advance DEI in business and other settings.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken GDRS2012.
courses.learning_outcomes:
* Evaluate the aspects of your identity and personal experiences that shape how you interact and engage with others, as well as how others interact and engage with you in organizations.
* Explain how issues of power, privilege, discrimination, bias, equality, and equity influence op-portunity and effectiveness in organizations.
* Propose ways to make relationships across differences in organizations more effective.
* Describe current perspectives on the relationships among diversity and inclusion in global or-ganizations
* Describe current perspectives on the relationships among diversity, inclusion, creativity, and in-novation in organizations.
* Analyze a company's current approach to leading diversity and use content from this course to propose ways to enhance learning and effectiveness in that company.
* Become familiar with best practices and policies for creating more inclusive work environments.

courses.description:
(UGEC3227 is double-coded with GDRS3021.)

This course critically explores the development of LGBTQI+ studies as an interdisciplinary field of inquiry. It traces how LGBTQI+ communities develop against the backdrops of social forces and social oppression. It analyzes the cultural, social and legal environments that LGBTQI+ people live with, in diverse spheres such as families, education, employment, health and the media. The discussions focus on both the structural constraints that LGBTQI+ people face, but also the agency and resilience they display. It explores the development of contemporary LGBTQI+ identities and social movements, as well as the politics involved. The course also asks how gender and sexual diversity is understood in different parts of the world in an era of globalization. Students are also exposed to different practices, policies and laws that are related to LGBTQI+ people’s lives.

The Sustainable Development Goals vow to work towards a society where no one if left behind, particularly those who are most vulnerable and marginalized. In many parts of the world, LGBTQI+ people are historically amongst the most vulnerable and marginalized in society. This course will discuss the unequal treatment they face in cultural, social, political and legal systems and discuss how such inequality may be addressed.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon successful completion of this course, the student will:

- Students are expected to be able to debunk how sexualities and intimacies are shaped by larger social forces and therefore socially constructed.

- Students are expected to be able to analyze the social and historical development of LGBTQI+ identities and communities.

- Students are expected to gain an understanding of LGBTQI+ lives in contemporary societies from empirical examples in diverse spheres of life.

- Students are expected to be able to critically examine how sexualities intersect with other social categories, such as race, ethnicity, class, age and disability;

- Students are expected to critically assess social messages regarding sexualities and intimacies and question the extent to which they themselves agree or disagree with the conventional ‘definitions’ and ‘norms’ in society.

- Students are expected to be able to explore and assess different practice, policy and legal options that are related to LGBTQI+ people’s lives.
courses.course_syllabus:
- Introductions

This week provides an overview of the theories and content covered by the course.

- Same-sex attracted individuals and gender diverse people – anthropological, cultural and historical traditions to the social construction of sexuality – defining diverse sexualities as different and deviant

This week explores social oppression faced by sexual minorities and the growth of the early gay liberation movement.

- Social oppression and the emergence of the early gay liberation movement

This week explores social oppression faced by sexual minorities and the growth of the early gay liberation movement.

- Sexual identities: ‘ethnic group’ or social construction? Global/local identities? Queering identities?

This week explores some of the contemporary debates about the nature of sexual identities.

- Gender diversity and transgender

This week introduces students to the social, legal and political realities that transgender people face.

- Intersex

This week introduces students to the social, legal and political realities that intersex people face.

- LGBTQI+ and intimacy and personal life

This week explores the intimacy and personal life of LGBTQI+ people.

- LGBTQ+ and education and employment

This week explores the education and employment experiences of LGBTQI+ people.

- LGBTQI+, health and well-being

This week explores the health and well-being of LGBTQI+ people.

- LGBTQI+ and the media

This week explores how media represents LGBTQI+ people.

- Margins within the margins? LGBTQI+ and intersections

This week explores how LGBTQI+ identities intersect with other social categories.

- Where are we heading? LGBTQI+ politics? Rights and equality? Post-gay era? Beyond gender and sexuality? The utopia to aim for and the realities to live with

This week explores future directions of LGBTQI+ identities and politics and concludes the course.

courses.subject: GDRS
courses.title: Gender Biases in Education and the Workplace
courses.description:
(UGEC3225 is double-coded with GDRS3024.)

Educational and occupational accomplishments vary dramatically among individuals and profoundly affect lives. Importantly, gender is among the powerful individual characteristics that affect people's experiences in educational and work settings. Gender shapes youths' experience and outcomes of education across the globe and across history. This course is designed to introduce to students, with a critical mindset, important contemporary issues at the intersection of gender, education and jobs. The topics address the role of gender in school- and related preferences and achievements, with a focus on social and cognitive approaches. For example, what is quality education (SDG goal 4) and why is it important to evaluate it with a gender perspective? Do men and women really differ in their abilities, and if so, by how much? Is personal interest a better explanation of gender imbalances in occupations? What are the ways that biases exist in education and the workplace, and how do teachers, parents, and employers contribute to gender biases (SDG goal 5)? Can gender equality in performance and hiring be improved by removing stereotypes? The literature will cover childhood, adolescence as well as adulthood in order to provide insight into the age-related changes of the gender issues. Particular attention will be paid to issues that relate closely to daily life, both local and international. Empirical evidence will be reviewed to illustrate the gender biases and substantiate the explanatory theories. Potential interventions to address gender biases in education and the workplace will also be discussed.

courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Be aware of gender differences and similarities and gendered treatments in learning and occupational contexts in daily life

2. Be knowledgeable of the classical and latest research on gender in educational and occupational contexts and outcomes

3. Be more informed about the causes of gendered phenomena in educational and occupational contexts and how to deal with them constructively

4. Be able to reflect on how gender has affected themselves as a learner and achiever
courses.course_syllabus:
This course is designed to introduce to students, with a critical mindset, important contemporary issues at the

intersection of gender and education and achievement. Collectively, the topics address the gender-education nexus in academic, occupational, and social domains from childhood through adulthood. We will pay particular attention to issues that relate closely to daily life, both local and international. Theories and data will be drawn mainly from Psychology but will also come from other disciplines.

In terms of SDGs, the course is most relevant for goal #4 (Ensure inclusive and equitable quality education) and goal #5 (Achieve gender equality and empower the marginalised gender).

Major concepts and topics:

1. Quality education and equitable workplace

Open discussion and review impressions on education and occupational contexts locally and internationally to assess if our current education is inclusive and equitable, and if gender equality and marginalization of gender are still pertinent questions in our society. Why is it important to achieve gender equality and empower the marginalized gender?

2. Gender differences and similarities in educational and occupational achievement

This topic includes questions such as: Gender differences is a topic that has fascinated researchers and is a topic of daily conversation. Do men/boys really differ from women/girls, and how? How big are the differences? Does it depend on contexts?

3. Gender-related academic and occupational interests and goals

Some argue that the gender gaps in academic and occupational outcomes may not be entirely the result of differences in ability or prejudice, but differences in the interests and goals of men/boys and women/girls. Are there gender differences in what people prefer as study subjects and occupations? What do men and women look for in their jobs?

4. Gender socialization in schools

In modern society, we spend almost ¼ of our life attending school. While a lot of gender socialization occurs at home, the school is also an important ground for gender socialization. What messages do schools and teachers send to students? How does sexism occur at schools and what interventions have been tested?

5. Gender segregation at schools: The case of single-sex education

Single-sex schools exist in many countries over the globe. However, few may notice that such institutional segregation of gender in the context of education has been a matter of intense social and scientific debate in the West. What are the rationales for the setting up of single-sex education? Do they live up to the tests of scientific studies? What are the possible consequences of gender segregation at schools?

6. Motivation, stereotypes and engagement: Does gender matter?

On the personal level, various psychological mechanisms may be at work to produce gender differences in educational and occupational achievement. What are some of these mechanisms? For example, how does stereotype threat and self efficacy theories apply to create gendered phenomena to make education inequitable?

7. Gender biases in the workplace

Women’s status in the workplace has improved drastically over the decades. However, have we gotten rid of gender biases in the workplace? What are some of the ways that gender biases manifest in today’s workplace, personally or institutionally, explicitly or implicitly?

8. Interventions to address gender gaps in the workplace

If gender gaps still exist in the workplace, what can be done about it? For example, what interventions may be effective in narrowing the gender gap in STEM participation? Many companies have adopted a quota policy to increase their representation of women. What have research shown about the impact of having more women or just more diversity in groups?

The teaching and learning approach will be a mix of teacher-guided and student-centred activities. Lectures will introduce the background knowledge of each topic and review the required readings. Then, students will be engaged in active group discussions. Before class, students have to post intellectual questions concerning the assigned readings. They will also look for additional discussion materials (academic articles, news reports, blog posts, etc.) and share them in class during discussions and presentations. The teacher and tutor(s) will monitor and guide the discussions. Towards the end of the course, students will present a poster on a self-selected topic related to gender and education/achievement in a mini-conference, and write an essay on the same topic to conclude their learned knowledge and demonstrate their ability to critically analyse the topic.

courses.subject: GDRS
courses.title: Media Representations of Minorities in Hong Kong
courses.description:
(UGEC3215 is double-coded with GDRS3214.)

How do we understand the notion of “minority”? And how does “minority”, look like, in cinema?

This course examines the rich tradition of critical film theory and provides students with an understanding of its main concerns and debates, concerning “minority” identities and subalternity. Addressing key discussions about media representations of “minority”, the course will look at the politics of representation and its impacts, both on- and off-screen. We will be looking at how media works to construct “the Other” in different contexts, and delve into important work which have inspired minority cinema, subaltern cinema, avant-garde cinema, local cinema, and independent cinema among other counter-movements, as well as recent re-conceptualizations and exploration of representations marginalized identities, positions, and experiences, local and abroad.

This course aims to introduces students to cinematic representations of differences as a productive paradigm of identity politics. While cultural theorist Stuart Hall suggests that identity politics is primarily a representation politics enacted through ever-changing conjunctures of differences, black feminist scholars bell hooks remind us that the margin is an open-ended position to create inside-out and outside-in connections. To examine the representation of minorities is therefore to cultivate an ability to embrace the margins, which allows us to ponder how the construction of “we” can be opened to “they,” the differences. With its divergent forms such as video art, film activism, commercial cinema and film system as well as film festivals, cinema provides a powerful medium for such a task. The question asked throughout the course would be: what are the normative representations of minorities in different forms of cinema? At the production level, to what extent could “minority” artists and theorists rebel against such normative representations—with what alternative visual images and narrative tools? At the level of spectatorship and reception, what kind of reading strategy could generate alternative politics of the minorities? What happened when digital platforms intervene into such endeavors of the minorities?

In the course, we will look at how socially marginalized groups, such as gay and lesbians, transgender persons, ethnic minorities, women in different stages of their lives, people with mental illness, teenagers, the elderly and many others are portrayed, and seek to expand their on-screen existence in Hong Kong media. In the exploration, we will engage with key questions in critical film studies, including spectatorship, authorship, popular culture, character stereotypes in genre films, self-representation, systems of media production, pro-sumer culture, (g)local cinema, alternative cinema, and new media. Students would be introduced to trailblazing works by “minority” filmmakers and media creators worldwide, and important cinematic and media movements that challenge the status-quo. Special focus will be placed onto important works in Hong Kong and Asia for critical examination and comparative studies.

Through critical exercise and reflection on notions such as power, diversity, equality, and freedom, the course aligns with the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) adopted by all United Nations Member States in 2015 by engaging directly with the SDGs 5 (Gender Equality) and 10 (Reduced Inequalities).

courses.course_syllabus:
1. What is Representation?: A View of the Margins (SDG 5 & 10)

- Setting up the tone of the course by introducing theories and concepts in relation to representations and margins

2. Hong Kong and the Minor (SDG 5 & 10)

- Introducing the idea of minor literature as a state of becoming to contest existing cultural identities and critical theories of Hong Kong

* *Part 1: Forms of Cinema as Media**

3. Engendering Hong Kong Cinema (SDG 5 & 10)

- Brief introduction of the history of Hong Kong cinema with a focus on the gendered and sexual meaning of its overt commercialism

4. A Feminist Reading of Hong Kong’s Independent Cinema (SDG 5 & 10)

- Investigating the ample representations and self-representations of gender and sexual minorities in the independent realm

5. Film Festivals: A Site of Social Actions (SDG 5 & 10)

- Explaining the significance of film festivals in enacting social changes for the minorities

- Introducing the way feminist and queer communities utilise film festivals to achieve their activist goal

* *Part 2: Figures of Minorities**

6. Sexualizing Hong Kong’s In-Betweenness (SDG 5 & 10)

- Examining how representations of sexual minorities construct Hong Kong cinema as a border that both separates Hong Kong from and connect it to East Asia

7. Coming-of-Age in a Postmetropolis (SDG 5 & 10)

- Reckoning with youth as both a status of identity and a representation that ascribes sexual and gender politics in a capitalist city

8. Aging Differently (SDG 5 & 10)

- Studying how the representation of aging serves as an analytical tool by which social inequalities in and the cultural identity of Hong Kong can be interrogated

9. Trans Cinema: Shimmering and Transing (SDG 5 & 10)

- Equipping students with conceptual tools such as aesthetic of shimmering and politics of trans-ing to critically engage with the debate between trans as an identity and non-identity politics

10. Ethnic Minorities: Constructing South Asian Men and Southeast Asian Women (SDG 5 & 10)

- Reflecting on the gendered and sexualized stereotypes of ethnic minorities and the very notion of ethnicity/race in Hong Kong

11. Crippling Cinema: Disability, Gender, and Sexuality (SDG 5 & 10)

- Learning from the representations of the disabled that how able-bodiness underpins our perception of sexualities

12. Are They Also One of Us? New Immigrants, Indigenous People, and Boat People (SDG 10)

- Questioning the boundary of Hong Kong identity by learning the history of the minorities excluded from the mainstream, popular, and official discourse

13. Queer Infrastructure: Mediating Life Within and Beyond Identity Politics (SDG 5 & 10)

- Introducing the queer conception of infrastructure as a living mediation of lifeworlds to prob with the possibilities of envisioning an open-ended Hong Kong’s future

courses.subject: GDRS
courses.title: Talking Across Differences: Compassionate Communication in Action
courses.description:
Words can keep one another at arm’s length, while bringing people together. This paradoxical nature, coupled with the fact that we cannot not communicate, makes our interpersonal lives moderately challenging and, more often than not, a source of joy, fulfillment, and connection, as well as frustration, unhappiness, and even conflict.

In the 1960s, having witnessed humanity's capacity for both violence and care, clinical psychologist Marshall Rosenberg sought to develop Compassionate communication (also known as Nonviolent communication) to foster love, truth, and courage. This model enables people to stay connected with themselves and others by identifying and expressing their underlying basic needs, even during times of conflict.

Not only does Compassionate communication nurture connection, but it also provides various tools to shift the mode of influence from power-over to power-with in collaborative settings, contributing to a peaceful and inclusive future. In fact, Compassionate communication has been recognized by UNESCO as one of the best practices for nonviolent conflict resolution in both formal and informal education worldwide.

As working with people from diverse backgrounds becomes increasingly common, the ability to communicate across differences is essential. This course will cover all the essential concepts, processes, and skills of Compassionate communication for both personal and social life. Through interactive learning in the form of role plays, demonstrations, and real-life scenarios, the course will cultivate students' awareness of their needs and their ability to authentically express themselves. Students will learn to listen with empathy, make life-enriching requests, apologize without self-blame, navigate conflict, hold difficult conversations, and facilitate collaboration across differences.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken GEWS2161.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. **Explain and differentiate** the key premises, processes, concepts, and skills of Compassionate Communication, including observation, feelings, needs, and requests, in relation to everyday interpersonal and social contexts.
2. **Apply and demonstrate** Compassionate Communication skills in structured exercises and real-life scenarios, including self-empathy, honest self-expression, empathic listening, and making life-enriching requests without demands.
3. **Identify, articulate, and regulate** their own emotions and needs, and attend and respond sensitively to the emotions and needs of others, showing increased empathy and emotional awareness in interaction.
4. **Communicate and collaborate** with confidence and care in difficult conversations and conflicts by co-creating mutually acceptable strategies, expressing disagreement without blame, and seeking outcomes that cater to the needs of all parties.
5. **Demonstrate openness and curiosity** toward differences in values, identities, and communication styles, and integrate Compassionate Communication principles to work constructively with people from diverse social and cultural backgrounds.
6. **Analyze and evaluate** real or case-based communication episodes (e.g. conflicts, microaggressions, or everyday misunderstandings) by identifying observations, feelings, needs, and requests, and by assessing the extent to which Compassionate Communication principles are embodied or violated.
7. **Design and sustain** a personal or collective practice plan for integrating Compassionate Communication into one’s daily life, studies, or community work, and reflect on changes in one’s attitudes, relationships, and communication patterns over time.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *CLASS 1
The Basics of Compassionate Communication**
This lecture introduces the origins of Compassionate Communication—developed by clinical psychologist Marshall Rosenberg in the 1960s—and outlines its key premises. Framed as a language modality that fosters dialogue, Compassionate Communication is contrasted with other common forms such as debate and persuasion, and its emphasis is placed on overcoming four common language patterns that hinder connection and understanding.

Central to the approach are its four core components—observation, feelings, needs, and requests—which enhance clarity and strengthen interpersonal connection. Students will learn to clearly distinguish between observations and evaluations, feelings and thoughts, needs and strategies, and requests and demands, while cultivating a “needs consciousness” that forms the foundation for empathetic listening.

* *CLASS 2
The Power of Empathetic Listening**
Empathetic listening goes beyond the mechanics of hearing words; it involves immersing oneself in the speaker's emotional landscape, understanding their feelings and needs, and responding with genuine care and understanding.

In this session, participants will learn to distinguish empathetic from non-empathetic responses listening, and the power of empathetic listening in transforming conflict to connection.

* *CLASS 3
Practicing Empathetic Listening**
Tuning into the inner world of another person is a skill that requires refinement through repeated practice. This session will provide students with four easy-to-follow frameworks for listening to others with empathy. It will also offer important advice on making empathetic guesses more colloquial and natural in daily conversations.

In empathetic responses, we often ask questions to gain a deeper understanding of the other person's experience. This session will distinguish between the types of questions that strengthen connection and those that do not.

* *CLASS 4
Making Life-Fulfilling Requests**
This week's topic focuses on making requests in Compassionate Communication, highlighting our interdependence in meeting needs through others. While making requests can feel daunting, the course examines how they invite contributions to our happiness and addresses challenges like fear of rejection and underestimating others' willingness to comply. We will explore barriers to making fulfilling requests and share strategies for clear communication to improve positive responses.

The session will also cover handling unmet requests and understanding the needs behind a "no." The goal is to help participants feel more comfortable asking for what they need while remaining open to any outcome.

* *CLASS 5
Expressing Appreciation and Making Apologies Without Self-Blame**
Expressing appreciation is often recommended by relationship coaches as a relational lubricant. However, through the lens of Compassionate Communication, our usual way of expressing appreciation can sometimes take the form of judgment, even if it’s positive. The issue with judgment—positive or negative—is its static language, which can cast others, and even ourselves, in stone.

* *CLASS 6
Troubleshooting: Clearing What Stands in the Way of Using Compassionate Communication**
Failure teaches success. In this lesson, we will address frequently asked questions and common pitfalls encountered on the journey of learning and practicing Compassionate Communication.

Understanding the challenges we face can transform setbacks into valuable learning experiences. We encourage you to engage actively by asking any questions related to communication and its daily-life applications.

Together, we’ll explore strategies to navigate these challenges, enhancing both your understanding and practical skills in Compassionate Communication. Your inquiries will help deepen our discussion and foster a supportive learning environment.

* *CLASS 7
In-Class Mid-Term Quiz**

* *CLASS 8
Navigating Cultural Differences in Communication Styles**
This week’s topic explores the intersection of culture and emotions within Compassionate Communication. We’ll examine how cultural backgrounds shape perceptions, expressions, and management of emotions, focusing on the differences between individualistic and collectivistic cultures.

This session will discuss how cultures define ideal emotions, prioritize emotional expression or suppression, and the impact on mental and physical health. Additionally, we will consider how cultural perspectives influence emotional interpretation, whether through individual expression or situational context.

Ultimately, the goal is to enhance our awareness of how culture affects emotional experiences and apply this understanding to our Compassionate Communication practice.

* *CLASS 9
Why Can’t You Understand?!: Talking Across Attachment Styles in Intimate Relationships**
Intimate relationships can bring immense joy, yet they may also involve pain. Often, we find ourselves unconsciously re-enacting the attachment patterns formed with our childhood caregivers or yearning for the love we once lacked within our families. As a result, we may expect too much from our partners or give too little in return.

This lecture bridges Compassionate Communication and attachment theory, helping you understand both your own and others’ attachment styles, and discover healthier ways to express yourself and to listen with empathy and care. Specifically, this lecture will address the following questions commonly related to intimate relationships:

- If I'm in a “situationship”, how can I confess my feelings to the person I love and confirm that we have mutual relationship goals?
- After dating for a while, my partner's passion seems to have cooled off. I feel lost and want to address the issue, but I am afraid to do so. What can I do?
- My partner is wonderful, but there is a habit that I find hard to accept. Can I ask them to change?
- Some say you should not expect anything from your lover because the more you expect, the more disappointed you will be. Is this true? If I still have expectations, how can I express them?
- I care for my partner, but their desire for intimacy exceeds my comfort level, and I feel a bit stifled. How can I communicate my feelings without rejecting their love?
- I've tried, but I don't think we're a good match. Is there a “nonviolent” way to break up?
- I'm in pain within my relationship. My partner threatens to break up whenever they're dissatisfied. I truly want to love wholeheartedly—do I have a choice?

* *CLASS 10
Dancing with Conflict (I): What and Why?**
Conflict is an inevitable part of life, and while it can be intense and overwhelming, it also presents opportunities for growth and understanding. This week, we begin our exploration of conflict through the lens of Compassionate Communication.

We will start by examining our own typical reactions to conflict and identifying our default modes of engagement. We will discuss how conflict arises from differing needs, values, and interpretations of situations, and how these differences can be opportunities to learn from each other.

This lecture introduces the idea that all actions, including those that trigger conflict, are attempts to meet basic human needs. By understanding the underlying needs and values involved, we can begin to shift from blame and judgment to connection and resolution.

* *CLASS 11
Dancing with Conflict (II): 9 Skills to Navigate Conflict**
Building on our understanding of conflict, this week we will explore the practical application of the "9 Skills" of Compassionate Communication developed by Jim and Jori Manske. These skills offer a framework for effectively navigating conflict, beginning with the vital "Zero Step" of being present and self-connected. We will emphasize the importance of self-empathy in managing reactions and remaining grounded.

The lecture will cover inviting others into dialogue, creating a safe space, expressing ourselves honestly, listening with empathy, making clear requests, and acknowledging different perspectives. We will also discuss using gratitude, feedback, and confirmation to enhance communication and understanding.

By applying these skills to past conflicts, we aim to build stronger relationships and navigate difficult conversations. Ultimately, the focus will be on gaining confidence in using these skills in daily life for more compassionate and effective interactions.

* *CLASS 12
Collaborating Across Differences: Compassionate Communication for Team Work**
Collaboration is widely regarded as an essential means and a long-cherished goal across various settings. In this session, we will explore the roles and types of facilitation, along with practical facilitation skills to enhance our collaborative efforts. We will guide you through the process of designing a meeting, including setting a clear purpose and desired outcomes, as well as selecting appropriate processes.

Additionally, we will have a brief discussion on alternative decision-making systems beyond majority rule, which are more likely to address basic needs without creating opposition.

* *CLASS 13
Harvesting Co-Learning Experience**

courses.subject: GDRS
courses.title: Thesis I
courses.description:
This course is intended to be the first part of a sequence of two courses that form a capstone of a student’s study in the programme. (For the second part, see GDRS4010 Thesis II; no unit would be awarded if GDRS4010 is not taken subsequently.)
The course provides an opportunity for students to undertake supervised study on a selected topic of interest. Students will be led to examine aspects of the selected topic, conduct a critical literature review and a systematic investigation of the topic they have chosen. Students are required to apply for ethics approval as applicable, collect and analyze data, regularly discuss with the teacher concrete findings in their research and submit written progress reports as scheduled by the teacher and submit a complete project report at the end of the term. Students will present their work in an academic mini-conference and also be assessed orally. The assessment will be based on the thesis document, the oral performance, and performance throughout the thesis process.

courses.subject: GDRS
courses.title: Gender, Sexuality and Race in Political Issues
courses.description:
The course will examine the relationship between the development of gender and sexual identities and that of the contemporary politics mainly in, but not limited to, Asian societies. We will look into how the contemporary politics and gender structures in the regions interplay and produce racial identities through the lens of gender. For example, the Korean-Japan and China-Japan dispute over issues of comfort women in World War II; Gender activism in Gaza and Arab Spring, the female suicide bombers in middle-East, and religious right-wing movements in Hong Kong and Taiwan. Students will learn how to examine the issues of gender and sexual identities and how they are deeply embedded within the global politics as well as local social mores.

Specifically, the course is divided into two parts. First of all, we will review the key theories of citizenship, war, democracy, nationalism, human rights and so on – through the lens of gender. Students are expected to learn the core theoretical debates and methods through reading key texts. The second part of this course will analyze these theories and debates through cutting-edge case studies. Topics will include comfort women, sexual violence in war, terrorism, female suicide-bombers and right-wing religious movements, human trafficking, grassroots feminist organizations, anti-war activism and so on. Students are expected to establish a more solid and critical range of views to examine these global issues and political controversies.
courses.learning_outcomes:
\* The ability to raise profound questions about social phenomena.
\* The ability to design research and analyzing a data to answer the question thus raised.
\* The ability to research both local and global including but not limited to politics, economy and culture.
\* The ability to analyze and possess the multicultural and interdisciplinary vision.
\* The ability of social practice: to learn the implications of sociological research for social change, political debates and innovation.
\* The ability to research the relationship between conception of peacemaking and social constructions of sexual and gender identities.
\* The ability to research and analyze the role larger political and social conditions play in perpetuating gender based harms, discriminations and exclusions.

courses.subject: GDRS
courses.title: Social Inequalities and Health: Towards Diversity and Equity
courses.description: The course examines how health experiences and outcomes shape and are shaped by intersecting social inequalities. Topics such as health care inequalities, the medicalization of society, and feminization of medical control will be critically examined.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Students are expected to learn to critically assess the influences of social inequalities on health behaviours and health outcomes.
2. Students are expected to gain understanding of different theories explaining social inequalities in health from empirical examples in health and medicine.
3. Students are expected to learn to draw links between their daily experience and observation, and conceptual issues.
courses.course_syllabus:
Week 1 Introduction to social science perspectives on health

Week 2 What is health?

Week 3 Health inequalities: what are they and why they should be addressed?

Week 4 Blaming the socially marginalized? What is the lay perspective on health?

Week 5 Material approaches to the sociology of health

Week 6 Durkheimian/ social –relational approaches to the sociology of health

Week 7 Problems with health as a profession

Week 8 Medical knowledge and social control

Week 9 Social constructionism and disabilities

Week 10 Poverty and housing stress

Week 11 HIV

Week 12 Changing and addressing inequalities in health: what is needed?

Week 13 Conclusions

courses.subject: GDRS
courses.title: Internship
courses.description:
This is an experiential course which combines theoretical knowledge with real-life practices in community organizations. As one of the capstone options for undergraduate studies in GDRS, before the start of the second year, students are required to complete a three-month supervised internship in an NGO which partakes in gender and sexuality-related services or advocacy work.
The course aims to provide students with an opportunity to put theory into practice, apply what they have learnt in Gender Studies to real-life situations, and critically reflect on theoretical issues through the experiences they gain during internship. Through practice, this course equips students with better knowledge of agencies working in the field of gender and sexuality, and the skills specific to their day-to-day operation and long-term development. Upon the completion of the course, it is hoped that students would emerge as mature and critical social actors who are well prepared to work with people from all walks of life and cope with interpersonal, organizational as well as occupational challenges in various work settings with integrity, responsibility and commitment.

courses.subject: GDRS
courses.title: Thesis II
courses.description:
The course is intended to be the second part of a sequence of two courses that form a capstone of a student’s study in the programme. (For the first part, see GDRS4005; 3 units would be awarded for successful completion of GDRS4005 and GDRS4010.)

The course provides an opportunity for students to undertake supervised study on a selected topic of interest. Students will be led to examine aspects of the selected topic, conduct a critical literature review and a systematic investigation of the topic they have chosen. Students are required to apply for ethics approval as applicable, collect and analyze data, regularly discuss with the teacher concrete findings in their research and submit written progress reports as scheduled by the teacher and submit a complete project report at the end of the term. Students will present their work in an academic mini-conference and also be assessed orally. The assessment will be based on the thesis document, the oral performance, and performance throughout the thesis process.

courses.subject: GDRS
courses.title: Thesis
courses.description:
The course provides an opportunity for students to undertake a supervised research project on a selected topic of interest. Students will be led to examine aspects of the selected topic, conduct a critical literature review, formulate research methods that gather data to address the selected research question, analyze the data (if empirical), and discuss the conclusions and implications of the findings. Supervisors are expected to be experts in any discipline who are able to supervise a research project that includes gender/sex as one of the major components.
Students are required to regularly discuss with the supervisor about their research and submit written progress reports as scheduled by the teacher and a complete written thesis of about 8,000 to 10,000 words in length (excluding tables, figures, bibliographies, and appendices) at the end of the year. Students will present their work in an academic mini-conference.

courses.subject: GDRS
courses.title: Introduction to Gender Studies
courses.description: This course introduces students to the main streams of the current academic discourse on gender theory and feminism. It will explore various schools of thought in feminism and relate theoretical frameworks to issues of current interest such as the ability of women to earn an independent income, social security, occupational segregation, women sexuality, body politics and discrimination against women in different fields of society. It also introduces students to some basic methodology in gender and feminist research.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Gender Studies, MPhil-PhD Gender Studies
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon successful completion of this course, students will be able to:

1. Identify and explain key issues and debates in contemporary gender studies and feminist scholarship.
2. Interpret and engage with foundational theoretical texts in the field.
3. Describe and distinguish between different perspectives within gender studies and feminist scholarship.
4. Compare and evaluate competing perspectives, providing clear, evidence-based justifications.
5. Apply and extend gender theories to analyse and theorize cultural issues.
